does: This paper establishes a formal category-theoretic framework for mechanistic interpretability by introducing compositional interpretability, which represents model explanations as pairs of commuting syntactic and semantic mappings. The core contribution is recasting interpretability as a constrained optimization problem that trades off faithfulness (how well the explanation predicts model behavior) against complexity (description length). The authors introduce compressive refinement, a systematic technique to restructure neural networks into progressively simpler components while preserving functionality, and derive a parsimony criterion proving that syntactic compression yields more concise, human-aligned explanations under specified conditions. They demonstrate that prominent existing mechanistic interpretation methods emerge as special cases within their framework.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability research program, which seeks to reverse-engineer neural network computations into understandable components through techniques like circuit analysis and feature attribution. The paper responds to a fundamental limitation in the field: the absence of rigorous criteria for comparing and verifying explanations objectively. It draws on compositional semantics and category theory to formalize how model components can be decomposed and recomposed, incorporating principles from minimum description length and algorithmic information theory. The framework integrates insights from prior work on model compression, factorization, and the relationship between model structure and human understanding, while positioning itself as a unifying foundation for scattered mechanistic methods.
enables: This framework opens avenues for systematic model simplification and abstraction discovery in neural networks, potentially yielding genuinely understandable model descriptions rather than post-hoc explanations. By formalizing the tradeoff between faithfulness and simplicity, it enables principled research into which levels of abstraction best match human cognition. The compositional structure facilitates hierarchical interpretation where explanations can be verified and composed across architectural layers, supporting more rigorous machine learning auditing and safety analysis. Future work can leverage the framework to develop automated discovery algorithms for optimal decompositions, apply it to larger models and more complex behaviors, and test whether theoretically simpler explanations align with human understanding in practice.
